seoTitle: Record the Sale of a Fixed Asset in QuickBooks Online
metaDescription: Retire a sold vehicle, machine, or equipment from your books correctly with a balanced journal entry in QuickBooks Online.
intro: Selling a business asset means more than pocketing the cash; it requires removing the asset and its depreciation from your books and recording any gain or loss. This guide shows how to structure that adjustment as a balanced journal entry in QuickBooks Online.

steps.heading: Review your asset records
steps.body: Go to Chart of Accounts and check your existing Fixed Asset and Accumulated Depreciation rows for the item being sold.

steps.heading: Open a new journal entry
steps.body: Click + New, select Journal Entry, and prepare to record four balancing lines.

steps.heading: Zero out depreciation and the asset
steps.body: Debit Accumulated Depreciation for the total depreciated amount, then credit the Fixed Asset account for its original purchase price.

steps.heading: Record the cash received
steps.body: Debit your checking account for the actual cash received from the sale.

steps.heading: Balance the gain or loss and save
steps.body: Let the Gain or Loss on Sale of Asset account absorb the balancing difference, add notes for your audit trail, then save and check your Balance Sheet.

faq.question: Why do I need four lines in this journal entry?
faq.answer: You need to remove both the original asset value and its accumulated depreciation, record the cash received, and capture any resulting gain or loss to keep the entry balanced.

faq.question: How do I know if I had a gain or loss on the sale?
faq.answer: Compare the cash received to the asset's net book value (original cost minus accumulated depreciation); the difference determines gain or loss.

faq.question: How do I verify the asset was removed from my books?
faq.answer: Run a Balance Sheet report after saving and confirm the sold asset's line has dropped to zero.

faq.question: Should I add notes to the journal entry?
faq.answer: Yes, appending a clear memo across all lines helps maintain an audit trail for your accountant.
